figures.caption: Figure 1: (a) DEPT maintains high outcome entropy while baselines collapse into deterministic distributions. (b) DEPT sustains effective gradient signals while baselines suffer from gradient vanishing. (c, d) DEPT achieves superior performance, effectively addressing policy degeneration in open-ended social language games.
Figure 4: Training dynamics on Two Dollars. For Don’t Say it and Negotiation, please refer to Figures 6 and 7.
Table 2: Out-of-distribution evaluation on increased complexity games.
Figure 6: Training dynamics on Don’t Say It.

Figure 2: Training dynamics of the self-play RLVR in social language game (Negotiation). While (a) training Reward and (b) average Game Length exhibit normal growth patterns, indicating the acquisition of basic game mechanics, (c) for baselines, the Win Rate against a fixed Gemini-2.0 opponent suffers a significant collapse.
Table 6: The results of significant tests.
Table 3: Out-of-distribution on reasoning benchmarks. The best results are bolded. The second results are underline.

Figure 3: Overview of DEPT. The framework comprises two components: (1) Time-Scaled Evolutionary Perception, which maintains fast and slow EMA baselines, combined with Match Entropy to derive the Intervention Coefficient λ(t) quantifying evolutionary impasse; and (2) Asymmetric Advantage Reshaping, which leverages historical value bounds to suppress dominant outcomes and amplify rare trajectories, restoring gradient signals for continuous policy evolution
Table 1: Main experimental results on three adversarial games. The best results are bolded.
Table 4: Hyperparameter configurations used in all experiments.
